In the past I have used Thibaut Despoulain’s <a class="z ow" href="http://bkcore.com/blog/3d/webgl-three-js-volumetric-light-godrays.html" rel="noopener ugc nofollow" target="_blank">(WebGL) Volumetric Light Approximation in Three.js</a> with some updates to get it running in the latest version of three. This time I wanted to dig in and try an implementation directly from the original source, Kenny Mitchell’s <a class="z ow" href="https://developer.nvidia.com/gpugems/GPUGems3/gpugems3_ch13.html" id="g-recaptcha"></div></div></div><p id="28c2" class="pw-post-body-paragraph np nq hp nr b ns nu nv nw ny nz oa oc od oe og oh oi ok ol uy om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">The general technique remains the same across all the implementations:</p><ol class=""><li id="3c96" class="np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om ox oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">Render the scene, with the light source as white and all occluding objects as black, to a texture.</li><li id="4636" class="np nq hp nr b ns pa nu nv nw pb ny nz oa pc oc od oe pd og oh oi pe ok ol om ox oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">With a shader, apply a parameterized radial blur to that texture to create the lighting effect</li><li id="5556" class="np nq hp nr b ns pa nu nv nw pb ny nz oa pc oc od oe pd og oh oi pe ok ol om ox oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">Additively blend that result over a render of the normally lit scene and display on screen</li></ol><figure class="pf pg ph pi pj nb"><div class="nm gf e fw"><div class="pk no e"></div></div></figure><p id="3fe0" class="pw-post-body-paragraph np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">All the magic comes from the shader. In the Gems article Mitchell gives a detailed explanation of the math used ( most of which I don’t understand ) and how he ends up, finally, at his solution to</p><blockquote class="pl pm pn"><p id="715f" class="np nq po nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">…estimate the probability of occlusion at each pixel by summing samples along a ray to the light source in image space. The proportion of samples that hit the emissive region versus those that strike occluders gives us the desired percentage of occlusion…</p></blockquote><p id="99a9" class="pw-post-body-paragraph np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">He then adds a series of parameters to this summation to control the effect produced.</p><ul class=""><li id="4560" class="np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om pp oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph=""><strong class="nr hq">exposure</strong> controls the overall intensity / brightness.</li><li id="3068" class="np nq hp nr b ns pa nu nv nw pb ny nz oa pc oc od oe pd og oh oi pe ok ol om pp oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph=""><strong class="nr hq">decay </strong>controls the fall off from the light source.</li><li id="3afe" class="np nq hp nr b ns pa nu nv nw pb ny nz oa pc oc od oe pd og oh oi pe ok ol om pp oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph=""><strong class="nr hq">density </strong>controls the separation between samples. If the density factor is increased, the separation between samples is decreased, resulting in brighter light shafts covering a shorter range.</li><li id="a0ea" class="np nq hp nr b ns pa nu nv nw pb ny nz oa pc oc od oe pd og oh oi pe ok ol om pp oy oz bg" data-selectable-paragraph=""><strong class="nr hq">weight </strong>provides a secondary, fine grained control over the brightness.</li></ul><p id="6291" class="pw-post-body-paragraph np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">Each of these becomes a uniform in the shader that can be used to manipulate the effect. For my implementation I also included a uniform to adjust the number of samples along the ray, up to a max of 100. Both to see how the effect is built up and to tune the performance. A higher number of samples makes for a more expensive shader and slower frame rates.</p><figure class="pf pg ph pi pj nb"><div class="nm gf e fw"><div class="pk no e"></div></div></figure><p id="b1a8" class="pw-post-body-paragraph np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">To improve performance Mitchell recommends downsampling the effect by rendering it at a smaller scale than the screen and the blurring the result. For the sake of clarity I’m doing the scaled rendering but leaving out the blur.</p><p id="69c6" class="pw-post-body-paragraph np nq hp nr b ns nt nu nv nw nx ny nz oa ob oc od oe of og oh oi oj ok ol om hi bg" data-selectable-paragraph="">Additionally, if you don’t like the streaking artifacts that are produced he recommends reducing the contrast in the occlusion scene. In his example images the sun is a mid gray sphere.</p></div></div></div></div></section></div></div></article></div><div class="v cf"><div class="cm bd gu gv gw gx"><div class="pq pr v jm fy"><div
